name: The Drive — turn-by-turn for a circuit
oneLineEssence: The canvas drives the tech through the harness like a navigation app: on each step it physically glides to frame the one circuit that step needs, eases the rest of the truck off-frame to a faint background, and is never more than a tap from the whole map — the camera narrates the diagnosis, the layout never moves.
feltExperiencePitch: You pull up the 2018 F-250 6.7L on the P0087 walk. The screen isn't a page with a diagram in it — it IS the diagram, edge to edge. The walk's current step is "back-probe the lift pump power feed, key-on," and the canvas already knows that: it has glided in and parked the Electric Lift Pump, its connector, the 12V power pin, the ground, and the upstream Lift Pump Relay right in front of you, slightly toward the top-left so there's open room below. The CP4.2 pump, the eight injectors, the PCM, the FRP Regulator are all still there — you can see the faint shapes of the rest of the truck off to the edges — but they've eased back to a quiet wash so they're not shouting at you while you work one circuit. You put your finger on the lift pump's 12V pin, and a slim card rises from the bottom edge with the one thing you're chasing first: "Key-on — 12V briefly during prime, then 0V," set right against "Expected: 12V when commanded." The camera doesn't lurch — tapping never moves the truck out from under you. When you've logged that reading and the walk advances to the FRP sensor, the canvas does what a nav app does at a turn: it never blinks to black, it just smoothly leans over to frame the sensor's signal/5V/low-ref circuit, the lift pump easing back into the faint wash behind you. The whole thing feels less like browsing a schematic and more like being driven straight to the part — you never decide what to look at, the tool takes you there, and the whole truck is one "show whole system" tap away whenever you want to get your bearings.

┌────────────────────────────┐
│←  F-250 6.7L · P0087   ⌖    │ ← 44px ribbon: vehicle+code, ⌖ whole-system chip
│ Key On · Engine Off    ▾    │ ← scenario chip (tap → scenario sheet)
├────────────────────────────┤
│                            │

│                            │
├────────────────────────────┤ ← canvas pans UP so pin stays
│ ● 12V POWER    theory · why?│   visible ABOVE the sheet
│ NOW  12V during prime → 0V │ PEEK detent (~150px, no scrim)
│ EXP  12V when commanded    │ step-led reading leads.
│ ┌────────────┐ ┌─────────┐ │
│ │Logged · next│ │needs fld│ │ ← thumb-zone actions
│ └────────────┘ └─────────┘ │
│ ⌃ drag up: connector·theori│ ← secondary facts below fold
└────────────────────────────┘
focusFadeMechanic: Two layers, run together on one shared ~500ms ease-out, triggered ONLY by step-advance (never by tap). Layer 1 — CAMERA: each walk step declares a stepNodeIds set; for the representative step that's [sd4-67psd-lift-pump, sd4-67psd-lift-pump-relay] plus sd4-67psd-frp-sensor as the faded downstream anchor. On step entry, call `reactFlowInstance.fitView({ nodes: stepNodeIds.map(id => ({id})), duration: 520, padding: {top:0.18,right:0.32,bottom:0.34,left:0.20}, maxZoom: 1.15, interpolate:'smooth', ease: easeOutCubic })`. The dagre layout (rankdir TB, PCM as hub, NODE_WIDTH 210 / NODE_HEIGHT 86) never changes — only the viewport moves. The asymmetric bottom/right padding parks the framed circuit toward upper-left so the pull sheet's corner is empty (spine rule 5). maxZoom 1.15 stops a 1–2 node step from ballooning and severing its wires from their faded neighbors (the Maps "zoom out for context" lesson). Layer 2 — RECEDE: every node + edge NOT in stepNodeIds cross-fades to a new token `--vt-recede: 0.13` (just under the existing `.wire.off` 0.16, so faded structure reads quieter than a live-but-off wire); their dash-flow animation freezes (extend the existing `.wire.off { animation:none }` rule to all receded wires). Never `display:none` — the faded harness is the "you're still in the whole truck" anchor (aviation declutter-mode, ForeFlight airspace dimming). The fade PERSISTS across taps within the step and only re-resolves on advance (Altium persistent mask), so the canvas never strobes as the tech inspects parts. Under `prefers-reduced-motion: reduce`: identical destination framing but `duration: 0` (instant cut) and the opacity cross-fade kept — camera pans are the top vestibular trigger, so the tween is the ONLY thing dropped.
tapToPullBehavior: Tapping the lift pump's 12V pin (a >=48px target) does NOT move the camera — selection is decoupled from navigation (the Figma Zoom-to-Selection rule). A bottom-anchored sheet rises into the empty corner the asymmetric padding reserved, ~280ms ease-out, with a hairline leader line drawn from the actual pin to the sheet's top edge (Figma measurement-feedback style) so the detail reads as anchored to THIS part, not a detached aside. The sheet's form: desktop = a slim panel docked to the canvas bottom edge (ZEUS "live meter in the lower portion" model), never the old right `<aside>`; it covers ~32% of canvas height max, never the framed pin above it. It is pull-on-tap, one-part, every-word-counts — the moment it became persistent it would re-violate the theory-of-operation-is-source rule, so it dismisses on background-tap (the existing `onPaneClick` already wires this) or the ✕. Tapping a DIFFERENT framed part swaps the sheet content in place (no camera move). Tapping a FADED part (e.g. the CP4.2 pump) is a first-class gesture: it "peeks" that one part from the 0.13 recede floor up to ~0.55 and raises its sheet, WITHOUT changing the step or moving the camera — so the tech can sanity-check "is the CP4 downstream of this?" then dismiss and the part settles back. Every sheet open begins on pointerdown, not click-resolve, because all data is already in memory (loadSystemTopology's useMemo'd Maps) — the canvas leans toward the part before the finger lifts (Linear optimistic-UI sub-100ms).
stepAwareOrdering: The SAME tap on the lift pump's 12V pin is ordered by where the walk is. Mid-test on "back-probe the lift pump power feed, key-on," the sheet LEADS with the live reading for THIS test set against expected (PicoScope reference-vs-actual): "NOW — 12V briefly during prime, then 0V" stacked directly under "EXPECT — 12V when commanded (key-on prime / cranking / running)" so a wrong value is SEEN, not parsed. Second tier (present, dimmer, one line down): the connector ("power wire at lift pump connector, under truck near tank"), pin count (2 pins: 12V Power, Ground), what each wire does, location ("driver-side frame rail"). The failure tree (missingLogic: "0V when commanded → probe upstream: relay, fuse, driver module; 12V but pump silent → ground or motor") stays collapsed behind a "⌄ connector · 2 pins · Operational Theori" disclosure until the reading is actually out of range — you don't pre-load the failure branch onto a test that hasn't failed (expertise-reversal: redundant for a master tech). The short Operational Theori snippet (drawn from `components.role`/`wireSummary`/`body`, NOT the architecture-facts theory rows) lives at the bottom of that disclosure. When the walk advances to the FRP Sensor step, the same tap on the FRP signal pin leads instead with "NOW — Flat low even while cranking — this is what the fault is showing" against its 0–5V expected band; the lift pump's reading is no longer the lead because it's no longer the moment. One tap, answer re-ordered by the step (NN/g contextual progressive disclosure).
provenanceTreatment: Provenance is drawn INTO the part and the reading, never stamped as a pill (the rejected verdict-pill / badge-wall pattern that today's amber `.topo-node__gap` chip is). Three quiet registers, grounded in ISO 2575 / SAE J578 dashboard semantics the tech already reads: (1) "field-verified" → the part draws at full ink weight, with a single hairline navy `--vt-signal-500` tick; (2) "inferred from theory" (the default for these TRAINING-CONFIRMED/INFERRED parts) → drawn at slightly lighter / desaturated stroke and a neutral graphite (`--vt-fg-3`) mono micro-caption — no color, because it's the baseline state, not an alarm; (3) "needs field check" (GAP) → a hairline DASHED edge on the part (the universal "provisional / not-to-scale" schematic convention) plus a saturated amber (`--vt-amber-500`) mono micro-label. Amber is the ONLY provenance state allowed to raise salience, and only at the moment of a probe — because an unverified value the tech is about to act on genuinely changes whether they trust the number. Red (`--vt-risk-high`) is RESERVED for an actual measured-wrong/fault reading (the fault-low FRP state), never spent on a data gap, so the tech's eye learns: red = measured-wrong, amber = we-don't-know-yet, ink-weight = how-sure-we-are-of-the-drawing. The grade word itself ("inferred from theory") is the see-source affordance — it carries no separate icon.
scenarioStateTreatment: Scenario state lives in the recessed top ribbon, not a competing block. The current 4-group ScenarioBar (Key/Engine/Load/Fault) collapses to a single low-contrast reading in the ribbon: "Key On · Engine Off" — mono, dim, with the canonical kind/sub derivation (deriveCompound / findOperationSlug logic) unchanged behind it. The live "now showing" is not a separate strip; the active scenario's reading surfaces only WHERE it's relevant — in the pull sheet's NOW line for the tapped pin (e.g. "12V briefly during prime, then 0V" under key-on; "12V steady — if pump silent, suspect ground or motor" under fault-low). A fault scenario tints the ribbon's scenario word amber→red and is the one case where the canvas's active wires animate at their true fault speed while the receded harness stays frozen, so motion itself reads as "this is the fault path." Changing scenario re-resolves the wire-state/reading layer (the existing scenario→pinReadings map) but does NOT move the camera — only step-advance reframes. The whole-system "⌖" chip (Maps showRouteOverview) sits at the ribbon's right edge: tap it and the camera `fitView`s to all nodes and lifts the dim to show the entire fuel harness; the next step-advance or tap auto-returns to the current step's framing (always the CURRENT step, never the stale one).
seeSourcePath: The provenance grade word in the pull sheet ("inferred from theory") is itself the affordance, with a quiet trailing "· why?" (mono, `--vt-fg-3`, >=44px touch target). Tapping it expands the why-we-believe-this inline, in place, below the reading — then collapses; never a standing "sources" column, never a modal. The expansion is short and every-word-counts (it would re-cross into the rejected push pattern if it became a dump): it surfaces the `labelGap` honesty already in the data ("Wire color, connector pin number, prime duration, and upstream relay/fuse identifiers — not yet captured") and, for a GAP part, the amber "needs field check" reasoning. This mirrors the part-detail grammar exactly — nothing speaks until asked, and when asked it answers in place and gets out of the way — so the whole tool reads as "evidence with a source you can check," never an oracle, without ever using the word "AI."
visualLanguage: Spend the existing "Workshop Instrument" tokens (app/globals.css + components/vt/v2.css + v2-instruments.css) wholesale — do NOT mint a parallel palette. REUSE: bone surface ramp (`--vt-bone-50` page → `--vt-bone-900` ink), single navy accent `--vt-signal-500` for focus/selection, ignition `--vt-amber-500` for the gap signal only, the three-face discipline (Instrument Serif for part names + the one-line Operational Theori; JetBrains Mono with `font-feature-settings:'tnum'` for EVERY reading/expected/pin number so digits don't jitter — cite `.vt-mono-big` and `.vt-stat__num` in v2-instruments.css; Inter Tight micro-caps for the ribbon eyebrow only), small radii (`--vt-radius-1` 2px), calm motion tokens (`--vt-ease`, `--vt-dur-2` 160ms / `--vt-dur-3` 280ms). ADD, in priority order: (1) DEFINE the six `--role-*` wire tokens that topology.css references but are undefined ANYWHERE (confirmed — wires render colorless today): `--role-12v` → warm red near `--vt-risk-high` (power=hot), `--role-ground` → `--vt-fg-2` graphite (return=neutral), `--role-signal` → `--vt-elem-voltage` chartreuse, `--role-5v-ref` → `--vt-elem-coolant` cyan, `--role-low-ref` → desaturated coolant, `--role-pwm` → `--vt-elem-vacuum` violet. (2) `--vt-recede: 0.13` as the canonical faded-background opacity (nodes/wires/labels). (3) REPLACE the heavy 1.4px-bordered `.topo-node` card with an engraved faceplate: thin 0.5px hairline (`--vt-rule-strong`, matching every vt divider), serif name 15–16px, mono micro-cap kind/location, pins as the only high-contrast geometry; selection = navy ink halo + `--vt-signal-500/0.10` tint, NOT the current 2px amber border (amber is overloaded — reserve it for "needs field check"). KILL react-flow's default dotted `<Background />` and default `<Controls />` (the literal rejected default-AI look) — use the kit's near-invisible bond-paper grid instead. Density: focused frame is information-rich (pin role-abbrev + wire-function labels); the whole-system frame sheds pin text to component names only (ForeFlight materialize-on-zoom).
appWidePropagation: Once the canvas proves this, three things propagate. (1) The camera-narrates-the-step pattern becomes the house model for any guided flow in the app — the intake wizard and follow-up flows reframe a single working surface per step instead of paging between full-screen forms, killing the "web page with a widget" feel app-wide. (2) The provenance-as-ink-weight ramp (full ink = field-verified, lighter = inferred from theory, dashed+amber = needs field check, red reserved for measured-wrong) becomes the canonical trust language everywhere the system shows drafted-vs-verified data — the research/curator surfaces, the cached-lookup verdicts — replacing every pill/badge with a quiet, consistent grammar. (3) The six `--role-*` wire tokens and `--vt-recede` get hoisted into globals.css `:root` as first-class semantic tokens, so any future schematic, harness reference, or system-data viewer inherits the same dark-mode-proof electrical-role color contract and the same focus/recede mechanic for free.
motionSpec: Three timed beats, all on `--vt-ease` (cubic-bezier(.4,0,.2,1)), no bounce, no overshoot (the OP-1 / Linear "don't perform" bar). (1) STEP REFRAME (the signature move): single ~520ms ease-out `fitView` camera glide to stepNodeIds + a simultaneous ~480ms (`--vt-dur` long end) opacity cross-fade of off-step nodes/wires to 0.13 — the two layers share the start and easing so the eye reads ONE motion ("the truck leaned toward the lift pump"), never a layout reshuffle. The diagram NEVER blinks to black between steps (Google Maps maneuver continuity — the deepest perceived-performance + trust signal). (2) TAP → SHEET: ~280ms (`--vt-dur-3`) ease-out rise from the bottom edge + the leader line drawing in; the tapped part gets a single decisive focus state (subtle navy halo + ~1.02 scale, Things-3-confident, not a soft web hover), begun on pointerdown. (3) RECEDE/PEEK SETTLE: faded parts EASE to the floor (a damped settle, never a hard opacity flip — a jerky cut reads as a glitch the way a fluttering gauge needle erodes trust); peeking a faded part rides the same ease from 0.13→0.55 and back. Wire dash-flow keeps animating at its true electrical-state speed ON THE FOCUSED CIRCUIT ONLY; receded wires freeze (extend the existing `.wire.off` rule). Under `prefers-reduced-motion: reduce`: camera `duration:0`, no pan/zoom, keep only the opacity cross-fade — same destination framing, same focus, same trust, motion stripped.
citations: React Flow fitView nodes filter / animated viewport — the camera primitive already in @xyflow/react: https://reactflow.dev/api-reference/types/fit-view-options

Google Maps / Navigation SDK camera — followMyLocation ↔ showRouteOverview toggle = the 'show whole system' model + maneuver-continuity (never blinks to black): https://developers.google.com/maps/documentation/navigation/android-sdk/camera
Google Maps Immersive Navigation — smart zoom-out for context (the maxZoom clamp lesson): https://9to5google.com/2026/03/12/google-maps-immersive-navigation/
Mapbox Navigation route-line — casing/active-vs-inactive-leg styling for the highlighted circuit vs receded harness: https://docs.mapbox.com/android/navigation/v2/guides/ui-components/route-line/
ForeFlight Airspace Highlighting + Continuous Zoom — relevance-grade dim off the route (= off the step), declutter-by-zoom: https://foreflight.com/products/foreflight-mobile/maps/

Snap-on ZEUS Guided Component Tests — detail in the LOWER portion of the screen beside the connection, location→connector/pins→operation→live meter: https://www.snapon.com/DiagnosticsManuals/ZEUS%20NA/Content/ZEUS/ComponentTest1.htm

PicoScope Automotive guided tests — fixed reference reading the captured value sits against (expected-vs-actual): https://www.picoauto.com/library/picoscope-automotive-training/knowledge-base/reference-waveforms
KiCad net highlight — brighten the active net, dim all else (fade-not-delete is the pro-EDA convention): https://docs.kicad.org/9.0/en/pcbnew/pcbnew.html

Altium Cross-Select Mode — persistent mask during interactive editing (fade persists across taps, re-resolves on step change): https://www.altium.com/documentation/altium-designer/sch-pcb/cross-probing-selecting
Figma Zoom-to-Selection (Shift+2) — selection decoupled from navigation; tap never moves the camera: https://help.figma.com/hc/en-us/articles/360040328653-Use-Figma-products-with-a-keyboard
Linear 2026 refresh — recede orientation chrome a few notches dimmer; 'don't compete for attention you haven't earned': https://linear.app/now/behind-the-latest-design-refresh
prefers-reduced-motion guidance — pan/zoom are vestibular triggers, opacity fade is the safe substitute: https://web.dev/articles/prefers-reduced-motion
Apple UISheetPresentationController detents — non-modal peek/half (no scrim) so the canvas stays visible: https://developer.apple.com/documentation/uikit/uisheetpresentationcontroller/detent

Turo Engineering — shift the map camera up as a bottom sheet moves so the point of interest stays visible: https://medium.com/turo-engineering/adjusting-compose-google-map-while-bottom-sheet-moves-4a7465305137
ISO 2575 / SAE J578 telltale color hierarchy — amber=advisory/check-soon, red=stop-now (grounds provenance + fault color): https://blog.ansi.org/ansi/sae-j-578-2020-color-lighting-motor-vehicles/
Material Design touch targets / thumb zone — >=48px targets, primary actions in the bottom third: https://m3.material.io/foundations/designing/structure
risks: The whole bet lives in motion — under prefers-reduced-motion it degrades to an instant cut. The cut must still be GOOD (correct framing + opacity cross-fade), or reduced-motion users get a markedly lesser tool; test the duration:0 branch at 375px specifically since reduced-motion is disproportionately common on phones.

Camera reframe is the riskiest interaction to get wrong: reframing on tap (instead of only step-advance) would yank the truck out from under a tech mid-probe and induce motion sickness. The selection-vs-navigation decoupling is load-bearing and easy to regress.
A 1–2 node step (lift pump + relay) can over-zoom and sever the wires from their faded context if maxZoom isn't clamped and the downstream FRP anchor isn't kept in frame — defeats the spatial point.

The step model does not exist yet — the canvas is a flat empty|component|pin|connection explorer with zero step-awareness. 'The Drive' needs the wizard to emit stepNodeIds + a step type, which is net-new wiring between the walk and the canvas, not just a styling pass.
Fading too hard (toward vanish) trades cognitive-tunneling for disorientation — the 0.13 floor must stay locate-able and tappable (peek gesture), never display:none.
The persistent 'show whole system' overview can desync: if it's open while the walk advances in the background, returning must snap to the CURRENT step, not the one active when overview opened.

Mobile: the bottom sheet covering the just-tapped pin is the classic self-defeating bug; the camera-pan-up (Turo technique) must fire on every detent change or the whole 'never on top of the part' spine rule breaks on the smallest screen.
buildNotes: Spearhead is achievable against the existing @xyflow/react stack with no new dependency — the camera is one documented call. Concretely: (1) Add a step model that the wizard emits: { stepNodeIds: string[], stepType: 'orient'|'locate'|'probe'|'interpret'|'branch', leadPinId?: string }. The representative step = { stepNodeIds: ['sd4-67psd-lift-pump','sd4-67psd-lift-pump-relay','sd4-67psd-frp-sensor'], stepType:'probe', leadPinId: lift-pump 12V pin }. (2) Capture the instance via `onInit` (or useReactFlow) and replace the single mount-time `fitView` with a per-step `instance.fitView({ nodes: stepNodeIds.map(id=>({id})), duration: prefersReducedMotion?0:520, padding:{top:0.18,right:0.32,bottom:0.34,left:0.20}, maxZoom:1.15, interpolate:'smooth' })` in a useEffect on step change. (3) Recede: pass a `recededNodeIds` set into toFlowElements; add a node `.is-receded` class → opacity `--vt-recede`, and extend the existing `.wire.dim`/`.wire.off` mechanism so the recede is driven by STEP not just pin-selection (the CSS half already exists). (4) Define the six `--role-*` tokens + `--vt-recede` in globals.css (one-time, unblocks the colorless-wire bug). (5) Replace `<Background/>`+`<Controls/>` with the bond-paper grid + the custom '⌖ whole system' chip wired to a no-`nodes` fitView. (6) Detail surface: refactor the right `<aside>` into a bottom-docked sheet (desktop) / 2-detent persistent bottom sheet (mobile, non-modal until full) reusing the existing PinBody/ComponentBody content but re-ordered step-aware (lead = scenario.pinReadings[leadPinId] vs pin.expectedReading; secondary collapsed). (7) Mobile camera-pan-up on detent change (Turo). Data is 100% in memory already (loadSystemTopology useMemo Maps) so every interaction is zero-latency. Hardest net-new piece is the wizard→canvas step contract; everything else is reframing existing pieces.

check.direction: The Drive — turn-by-turn for a circuit

spineCompliance.diagramIsWorkspace: PASS. The canvas is edge-to-edge with zero competing siblings; the old .topo grid (canvas as one box beside scenario/readout/panel/footer) is explicitly replaced. Scenario state collapses into a recessed top ribbon, the detail is a transient bottom-docked sheet, captured/missing is curator-gated. Nothing sits permanently beside the diagram. Verified against topology.css .topo grid (lines 14-38) which it correctly identifies as the thing to kill.
spineCompliance.stepAwareOnlyWhatsNeeded: PASS, and this is the direction's strongest claim. stepNodeIds = [sd4-67psd-lift-pump, sd4-67psd-lift-pump-relay, sd4-67psd-frp-sensor] are all REAL slugs (verified in scene-data.json). The camera frames only the lift-pump circuit + keeps the FRP sensor as a faded downstream anchor; the eight injectors, CP4.2, PCM, FRP regulator (all real components) recede. The single piece of context that leads on tap is correctly the live reading for THIS test. Genuinely 'only what's needed now,' not a styled full-harness view.
spineCompliance.fadeNotVanish: PASS. --vt-recede:0.13 with explicit 'never display:none' and the faded harness justified as the 'still in the whole truck' anchor (aviation declutter-mode cited). The 0.13 floor sits just under the existing .wire.off 0.16 (topology.css line 290) so faded structure reads quieter than a live-but-off wire — a real, defensible numeric relationship, not an arbitrary value. Peek gesture keeps faded parts tappable. The honest risk (fading toward disorientation) is named in the risks array.
spineCompliance.tapToPullStepAware: PASS with one provenance-label defect (see guardrail). Tap is decoupled from camera (Figma Zoom-to-Selection cited) so a probe never yanks the truck. Sheet rises on tap, dismisses on onPaneClick (already wired, verified line 106) or close — never persistent, explicitly flagged as re-violating theory-of-operation-is-source if it became standing. Step-aware ordering is correct: NOW reading leads, connector/pins/Operational Theori secondary, failure tree collapsed until reading is out of range (expertise-reversal cited). 'Operational Theori' spelled with the 'i' throughout — never autocorrected.
spineCompliance.contextWithinCanvas: PASS. Scenario state in the recessed ribbon (not a competing block); the live 'now showing' is dissolved into the pull sheet's NOW line rather than a standing strip; the sheet is bottom-anchored into the empty corner the asymmetric fitView padding reserves, explicitly never covering the framed pin. The mobile camera-pan-up (Turo technique cited) keeps the tapped pin visible above the sheet — the one place 'never on top of the part' is hardest, and it's engineered, not hand-waved.
spineCompliance.curatorOnlyCompleteness: PARTIAL/UNDERSPECIFIED. The captured/missing X-of-Y summary is correctly absent from the technician canvas, and provenance is handled per-part (good). But the direction never explicitly states the captured/missing summary is curator-role-gated — it folds 'data-status' into the labelGap honesty and the see-source path, both of which are technician-visible. It neither violates the rule nor affirmatively confirms the curator-only gate. A curator-preview-mode treatment of the completeness summary is missing from the spec.

guardrailCompliance.noAiWord: PASS. No 'AI' anywhere in copy, badges, or labels. The seeSourcePath uses 'why?' and 'inferred from theory'; provenance is framed around the source/the grade word. Explicitly calls out 'never using the word AI' as a design goal. Clean.
guardrailCompliance.noStepNofM: PASS — and notably hard for a nav-app metaphor, which it threads. No counter, no 'step N of M', no upcoming-work preview. The camera 'narrates' the current step by framing; the next step is computed silently and only revealed by the reframe AFTER the tech logs the reading. The mobile 'Logged · next' action button is the only forward-reference and it shows no count or preview of what's next. Survives scrutiny.
guardrailCompliance.mobilePasses: PASS. This is real degradation, not an afterthought: 44px ribbon, 48px pin targets (Material thumb-zone cited), non-modal sheet with peek detent (~150px, no scrim, Apple detents cited), camera-pan-up on every detent change so the pin stays visible (Turo cited), reduced-motion branch tested specifically at 375px (named in risks, since reduced-motion skews mobile). The current mobile CSS (topology.css lines 695-770) is inline-stacked and crude; this is a genuine upgrade. The bottom-sheet-covers-the-pin classic bug is explicitly named and solved.
guardrailCompliance.provenanceHonest: DEFECT — fixable, not disqualifying. The representative-scene mockup labels the lift-pump 12V pin 'inferred from theory · why?'. But the ACTUAL data (scene-data.json) has that pin's sourceProvenance = TRAINING-CONFIRMED, which the label map (verified in topology-detail-panel.tsx lines 62-67) renders as 'from theory', NOT 'inferred from theory' (that string is reserved for TRAINING-INFERRED). Every lift-pump and FRP pin in the scene is TRAINING-CONFIRMED. The direction also loosely calls them 'TRAINING-CONFIRMED/INFERRED parts'. The provenance GRAMMAR is excellent and honest (ink-weight ramp, amber=we-don't-know, red=measured-wrong, dashed=provisional, grade-word-is-the-affordance, no pill); the bug is purely that the representative mockup mislabels the lead part's grade. A copy/data fix, but it must be corrected because provenance honesty is CORE and the flagship demo currently misstates it.
guardrailCompliance.notDefaultAiAesthetic: PASS, strongest of the guardrails. Explicitly KILLS react-flow's default dotted Background and default Controls (verified present today, topology-diagram.tsx lines 113-114 — the literal rejected look). No verdict pills (provenance drawn into ink-weight, not chips — it even names today's amber .topo-node__gap chip as the anti-pattern to remove). No emoji cards. Reuses the existing Workshop Instrument tokens wholesale rather than minting a parallel palette (all cited tokens verified to exist: --vt-signal-500, --vt-amber-500, --vt-risk-high, --vt-elem-voltage/coolant/vacuum, .vt-mono-big, .vt-stat__num, tnum). Engraved faceplate replaces the heavy 1.4px-bordered card (verified line 91). Citations are real URLs to real pro tools (Snap-on ZEUS, PicoScope, KiCad, Altium, ForeFlight, Mapbox, Figma, Linear).

principleViolations: Provenance mislabel in the flagship scene: lift-pump 12V pin shown as 'inferred from theory' but is TRAINING-CONFIRMED ('from theory') in the real data. Violates the provenance-honesty CORE constraint at the exact moment it matters most (the demo lead part).

Curator-only completeness (spine point 6) is neither confirmed nor specified — the direction handles per-part provenance well but never states the captured/missing summary is role-gated to curators and hidden from techs/owners, leaving a spine requirement unaddressed.
check.distinctnessVsOthers: Genuinely distinct. The Drive is the ONLY direction where the camera physically moves between steps — the signature move is a fitView glide that reframes the viewport while the dagre layout stays fixed. The Dimmer (harness never moves, only the light changes) is its nearest neighbor and shares the recede/fade mechanic, so there IS partial overlap on Layer 2 (the 0.13 cross-fade). But The Drive's differentiator is Layer 1, the camera glide; The Dimmer by definition keeps the camera still and only modulates opacity. The Meter foregrounds the reading as hero with the diagram as backdrop — a different hierarchy entirely. Risk: if the camera-glide were ever cut (e.g. reduced-motion makes it an instant cut), The Drive degrades toward The Dimmer's behavior — under prefers-reduced-motion it keeps only the opacity cross-fade, which is literally The Dimmer. So the two collapse into one for reduced-motion users. That is acknowledged in the risks but worth flagging as a distinctness seam.
honestRisks: The whole bet lives in motion. Under prefers-reduced-motion the camera glide becomes an instant cut and the direction degrades to opacity-only — i.e. it becomes The Dimmer. Reduced-motion is disproportionately common on phones, so a non-trivial slice of techs never see the signature move. The instant-cut framing must be GOOD on its own or those users get a markedly lesser, less-distinct tool.

The step model is genuinely net-new wiring (verified: today's selection is a flat empty|component|pin|connection explorer with zero step-awareness). The Drive needs the wizard to emit {stepNodeIds, stepType, leadPinId}. This is the hardest piece and is a contract between two systems, not a styling pass — the spearhead's value is gated on it existing.

The provenance mislabel (TRAINING-CONFIRMED shown as 'inferred from theory') is a small fix but it's on the demo's lead part, so a reviewer's first impression of the trust language is technically a lie. Must be corrected before this is shown to the founder, given how central trust-honesty is to the product.
Curator completeness gating is unspecified — if built as-is, the captured/missing summary could leak to technicians, violating spine point 6.
A 1-2 node step (lift pump + relay) can over-zoom and sever wires from faded context; the maxZoom:1.15 clamp and keeping the FRP anchor in frame are load-bearing and easy to regress.
The persistent 'show whole system' overview can desync if the walk advances in the background; returning must snap to the CURRENT step. Named, but it's real interaction-state complexity.
suggestedFixes: Correct the provenance label in the representative scene: the lift-pump 12V pin is TRAINING-CONFIRMED, so the sheet must read 'from theory · why?' not 'inferred from theory · why?'. Audit the mockup against scene-data.json so every grade word matches the real sourceProvenance per part (all lift-pump and FRP pins in the scene are TRAINING-CONFIRMED).

Stop describing the scene's parts as 'TRAINING-CONFIRMED/INFERRED' — they are uniformly TRAINING-CONFIRMED. Pick a real GAP part from the data to demonstrate the amber 'needs field check' state, otherwise the amber/dashed register is never actually exercised in the flagship scene (the labelGap honesty is present but that's a different register from a GAP-provenance part).

Add an explicit curator-only treatment: state that the captured/missing X-of-Y completeness summary renders ONLY for curator-access roles and is absent for technicians/owners, and show where it lives in curator-preview mode. Right now spine point 6 is unaddressed.

Specify the reduced-motion instant-cut framing as a first-class state, not a fallback — since it makes The Drive behave like The Dimmer, define what still distinguishes it (e.g. the destination framing and the leader-line anchor) so reduced-motion users still get a coherent tool.

Confirm the leadPinId for the representative step resolves to pin 6d88ec94-b7bd-4c53-8b17-706e280d983a (Lift Pump · 12V Power) and that the NOW line pulls scenario key-on reading '12V briefly during prime, then 0V' (verified present in pinReadings) — the spec should reference the real pin id, not just 'the 12V pin'.
